content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive-legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660         322-5660           322-5991         322-6641           322-6444

April 1, 1981


Carl K. Newton                                                A-81-41
City Attorney
City Hall
1400 Highland Avenue
Manhattan Beach, CA 90266

Re: Your request for opinion relative to financial interest
     definition.

Dear Mr. Newton:

     Thank you for your recent letter, however, we cannot
give specific advice unless the identity of the person, for
whom the advice is sought, is provided.¹  Nevertheless, I
can give you some general guidelines which may be helpful to
you in advising your Councilmember. You have related the
following facts:

     A member of the City Council of the City of Manhattan
     Beach is a Certified Public Accountant and is a share-
     holder in an accountancy corporation (CPA firm) holding
     shares constituting approximately 11% of the ownership.
     The CPA firm will be providing accounting services for
     a corporation which operates a restaurant. Yearly fees
     to the CPA firm will approximate $4,000 but may be as
     much as $6,000.

     Historically, the profits to the CPA firm have been
     about 30% of gross receipts after expenses. Accordingly,
     the Councilmember's income from the client operating
     the restaurant will be approximately $4,000 x 30% x
     11%, or $132 per year; assuming that the projected
     figures mentioned above are correct.

___________

     ¹/ See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(b)(1), which
reads in part: "The identity of the person whose duties are
in question shall be provided with the request for written
advice."

Carl K. Newton
April 1, 1961
Page Two


An individual, who is designated Mr. Z, is a 10% limited
partner in the restaurant corporation. Mr. Z is also a
developer of a certain property within the city which
is proposed for a tennis court complex. The tennis
court complex will be on property owned by the City and
leased to a tennis club operation of which Mr. Z will
be a principal.

Disqualification is required by Government Code Section
87100 in any case where the decision has a material financial
effect on a business entity or real property in which the
official has an interest, or on a source of income to the
official. As a general rule, an official is required to
disqualify himself or herself in matters which affect a
source of income to a business partner or associate only
where the matters also affect a source of income to or invest-
ment of the official. Whether a decision has such a material
financial effect on a financial interest of the Councilmember
is defined in our regulations.

On the facts presented, special attention should be
given to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)(B) and 18702(b)(3).
Also, your attention is directed to the following Commission
opinions: Thorner, 1 FPPC Opinions 198 (No. 75-039, Dec. 4,
1975) and Carey, 3 FPPC Opinions 99 (No. 76-087, Nov. 3,
1977) at pp. 102-105, copies are attached for your convenience.

If you or the Councilmember wish more specific advice,
or desire the protection of Government Code Section 83114(b),
for the Councilmember, then his or her identity will have to
be provided, as well as more specific facts.

If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:plh

2/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

3/ Ibid.

4/ Government Code Section 83114(b) provides for certain
immunity for persons who rely upon advice from the Commission,
but that advice must be specific as to specific individuals.
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b).

PHONE: 545-5621

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

CITY HALL - 1400 HIGHLAND AVENUE - MANHATTAN BEACH, CALIFORNIA - 90266

March 19, 1981


Mr. Ted Prim, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Mall
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Opinion Relative to Financial
    Interest Definition

Dear Mr. Prim:

This letter is written to request an opinion relative to an
interpretation of Section 87103 of the Government Code and
specifically subparagraph (c) of that section. The opinion
request is made relative to the following facts:

A member of the City Council of the City of Manhattan Beach
is a Certified Public Accountant and is a shareholder in an
accountancy corporation (CPA firm), holding shares constituting
approximately 11% of the ownership. The CPA firm will be pro-
viding accounting services for a corporation which operates a
restaurant. Yearly fees to the CPA firm will approximate
$4,000 but may be as much as $6,000.

Historically, the profits to the CPA firm have been about 30%
of gross receipts after expenses. Accordingly, the Council-
member's income from the client operating the restaurant will
be approximately $4,000 x 30% x 11%, or $132 per year; assuming
that the projected figures mentioned above are correct.

An individual, who is designated Mr. Z, is a 10% limited partner
in the restaurant corporation. Mr. Z is also a developer of
certain property within the city which is proposed for a tennis
court complex. The tennis court complex will be on property
owned by the City and leased to a tennis club operation of
which Mr. Z will be a principal.

Mr. Ted. Prim, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
Page 2
March 19, 1981


Does the fractional 10% interest by Mr. Z in the restaurant
corporation which is provided CPA services by the Councilmember's
CPA firm constitute a financial interest under the provisions of
Section 87103(c), which would prevent the Councilmember from
voting upon development matters related to the tennis club
development?

If I can provide any further information relative to this request,
please don't hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Carl K. Newton
CARL K. NEWTON
City Attorney

CKN:wu

sections.question: Does the fractional 10% interest by Mr. Z in the restaurant corporation which is provided CPA services by the Councilmember's CPA firm constitute a financial interest under the provisions of Section 87103(c), which would prevent the Councilmember from voting upon development matters related to the tennis club development?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a City Council member who owns 11% of a CPA firm providing accounting services to a restaurant has a disqualifying financial interest under Government Code Section 87103(c) based on a limited partner's 10% interest in that restaurant when the partner seeks city approval for an unrelated development project.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a city attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or advice section is present in this document. Document contains detailed factual scenario with complex ownership structure involving percentages and relationships. Some OCR issues visible in header but content is legible.

embedding.summary: The City Attorney of Manhattan Beach requests FPPC guidance on whether a Council member's indirect financial interest through a CPA firm creates a conflict of interest that would disqualify the member from voting on development matters involving a limited partner in the CPA firm's restaurant client.
